coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and fluorescein: The ideal FRET pair for the fabrication of a precise and fully reversible ammonia sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Hsu, C.-P.
Hejazi, Z.
Armagan, E.
Zhao, S.
Schmid, M.
Zhang, H.
Guo, H.
Weidenbacher, L.
Rossi, R.M.
Koebel, M.M.
Boesel, L.F.
Toncelli, C.
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring of ammonia in the gas phase, a ubiquitous bulk chemical, is of paramount importance to ensure the safety of industrial workers. The present paper describes a solid-state optical ammonia sensor based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between narrowly dispersed blue-emitting carbon nanodots (CNDs) as FRET donor and fluorescein as FRET acceptor. The fluorophores were physically entrapped in a close to superhydrophobic sol–gel matrix, in turn deposited on a PVDF-HFP electrospun fiber membrane. The CNDs were synthesized via pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetracetic acid (EDTA) in the presence of a hydrogen peroxide solution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed a high surface coverage of hydrophilic groups, which is a result the addition of hydrogen peroxide in the reaction mixture. This translates into higher electrostatic interactions between ammonia and the fluorophores, in turn related to reduced FRET inter-distance between fluorescein and CNDs. If calibrated with respect to the fluorescein signal, the sensor shows a linear calibration with a remarkably low limit of detection, i.e. 110ppb, and adequate reproducibility up to six N2/NH3 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ammonia sensor
Förster resonance energy transfer
Hydrophobic surfaces

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PVDF-HFP electrospun fiber meshes
Morphological properties of electrospun membrane and the resulting deposited sol–gel
Optical response of the solid-state ammonia sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM (A), particle size distribution as measured by HR-TEM (B), XPS deconvolution of C1s (C), O1s (D) and N1s (E), of the synthesized CNDs, including their excitation-dependent emission (F) and aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) (G) behavior.
Scanning electron microscopy of PVDF-HFP electrospun membrane before (A) and after (B) sol–gel deposition.

(A) Optical response of the CNDs emission peak to five different ammonia concentrations. The results were fitted to the equation: y =0.9535× x 0.4112 (R 2 =0.999). (B) Optical response of the fluorescein emission peak to five difference ammonia concentrations. The results were fitted to the equation: y =0.0026× x (R2 =0.999).
Ammonia sensor reversibility through six different N2/NH3 cycles [NH3]=50ppm.

Response time of the sensor calibrated on the fluorescein emission peak upon exposure to 100ppb, 500ppb, 10ppm and 20ppm, inset magnifying the optical response of the fluorescein emission peak upon exposure to 500ppb.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and fluorescein: The ideal FRET pair for the fabrication of a precise and fully reversible ammonia sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Empa, Swiss Federal Laboratories for Materials Science and Technology, Laboratory for Biomimetic Membranes & Textiles, Lerchenfeldstrasse 5, CH-9014 St. Gallen, Switzerland

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Empa-Swiss Federal Laboratories for Material Science & Technology, Applied Wood Materials, Überlandstrasse 129, CH-8600 Dübendorf, Switzerland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Empa-Swiss Federal Laboratories for Material Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ETH Zürich, Institute for Biomechanics, Department of Health Sciences and Technology, Hoenggerbergring 64, CH-8049 Zürich, Switzerland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Health Sciences and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring of ammonia in the gas phase, a ubiquitous bulk chemical, is of paramount importance to ensure the safety of industrial workers. The present paper describes a solid-state optical ammonia sensor based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between narrowly dispersed blue-emitting carbon nanodots (CNDs) as FRET donor and fluorescein as FRET acceptor. The fluorophores were physically entrapped in a close to superhydrophobic sol–gel matrix, in turn deposited on a PVDF-HFP electrospun fiber membrane. The CNDs were synthesized via pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetracetic acid (EDTA) in the presence of a hydrogen peroxide solution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed a high surface coverage of hydrophilic groups, which is a result the addition of hydrogen peroxide in the reaction mixture. This translates into higher electrostatic interactions between ammonia and the fluorophores, in turn related to reduced FRET inter-distance between fluorescein and CNDs. If calibrated with respect to the fluorescein signal, the sensor shows a linear calibration with a remarkably low limit of detection, i.e. 110ppb, and adequate reproducibility up to six N2/NH3 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia is a chemical precursor widely utilized in the synthesis of fertilizers, explosives, detergents, pharmaceuticals and other chemicals (e.g. nitric acid) [1]. Despite its widespread use, ammonia has pronounced toxicity and, at high exposure levels, it induces lung disease, liver failure and urea cycle disorder with consequent brain damage [2]. As a result, the European Agency for Safety and Health at Work has fixed occupation exposure limits for ammonia to 20ppm for 8h daily exposure and 50ppm for acute exposure. Similarly, the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) in the USA has set a 15min exposure limit for gaseous ammonia of 35ppm and an 8h exposure limit to 25ppm.

Hence, there is an impelling demand for a reversible, robust and accurate sensor able to monitor ammonia in the above mentioned concentration window (i.e. 10–50ppm). Several electrochemical sensors based on hybrid polyaniline composites [3–5], polypyrrole/phthalocyanine [6] or inverse opal [7] have already been developed for this purpose. Although they have shown remarkable limits of detection and an adequate operational window, they present the usual drawbacks associated with an electrochemical transduction, namely strong read-out interferences by other gaseous analytes. Optical sensors overcome the previously mentioned drawbacks and several fluorophores have been employed, such as eosin [8], tricyanofuran [9], trifluoroacetylazobenzene [10], up-converting nanoparticles [11], semi-conductor quantum dots [12] and carbon dots [13,14]. However, the simple readout by fluorescence leads to a considerable drift in signal according to the dye loading in the solid matrix, which jeopardizes the robustness and reproducibility of the sensor.

A possible solution to this conundrum presents itself with the sensor based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between two dyes with overlapping excitation/emission spectra. By calibrating the sensor via the ratio between FRET donor and acceptor emission peaks, any signal variation due to dye loading in the matrix is thereby eliminated [15–17]. Previous works of our group have exploited the possibility of physical entrapment for FRET pairs constituted by coumarin/fluorescein and fluorescein/rhodamine within sol–gel coatings [18,19]. The high hydrophobicity of the matrix allowed full sensor reversibility and these formulations were suitable to coat a wide range of substrates, including plates, optical fibers, and textiles. Moreover, we could demonstrate that the addition of mesoporous particles positively influenced the dye accessibility by ammonia gas [19]. Particles post-grafted with the fluorescent dyes showed better sensor performance in terms of reversibility and recovery. Unfortunately, the limit of detection (LODs) for this FRET system was only 115ppm [18], which is insufficient for ammonia monitoring in occupational environments.

In the last decade, carbon nanodots (CNDs) have emerged as a photostable, non-toxic and cost-efficient class of optical nanostructures [20,21]. Their tunable excitation-dependent emission characteristics render them particularly appealing for their use in FRET detection systems. Indeed, a FRET pair composed of carbon dots and sodium rhodizonate showed a remarkable limit of detection of 3ppm and a response time upon NH3 exposure of 5s [22]. However, reversibility of the sensor was only tested by adding hydrochloric acid, which is not viable for continuous monitoring of ammonia in occupational environments. Recently, an alternative bottom-up route has been presented for the synthesis of CNDs via carbonization of ethanolamine in the presence of hydrogen peroxide solution [23]. The role of hydrogen peroxide promotes surface defects and aids to increase the fluorescence quantum yield from 7% to 10.3%. In addition, the presence of an oxidant in the reaction media has proven to increase the concentration of hydrophilic groups on the surface and to reduce the overall reaction time from 2h to few minutes.

Here, we present an alternative route for the synthesis of CNDs by pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetracetic acid (EDTA) in the presence of a hydrogen peroxide solution. These CNDs, used as FRET donor, were embedded in a flexible sol–gel matrix in the presence of fluorescein as FRET acceptor to ensure the physical entrapment of the fluorophores. The sol–gel mixture was then drop cast on electrospun PVDF-HFP membranes to impart a highly porous morphology to the sol–gel layer, with the target of decreasing the response time for the resulting solid-state ammonia sensor. The feasibility of such an approach has already been proven successful in loading a porous polyolefin membrane with a solution of a luminescent oxygen-sensitive dye [24].

Fluorescein (for fluorescence, free acid), ammonium hydroxide (ACS reagent, 28–30% NH3 basis), methyltriethoxysilane (MTES) (99%), diethoxydimethylsilane (DMDES) (≥97%), ethylenediaminetetracetic acid (EDTA) (purified grade 99%). acetic acid (reagent plus ≥99%) and hydrogen peroxide 30wt.-% solution in water (Trace Select) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received without any further purification.

Compressed gas cylinders with selected NH3–N2 standard mixtures containing 100 ppb, 500 ppb, 10ppm, 50ppm, 100ppm, 150ppm and 200ppm of NH3 were obtained from Carbagas and employed for testing the sensor performance.

In a typical synthesis, 0.5g of EDTA was placed in a round bottomed flask with the addition of 3mL of 30wt.-% hydrogen peroxide solution. Afterwards, the reaction mixture was kept at a temperature of 250°C for 5min until the full evaporation of the liquid medium.

After quenching the solution to room temperature with a water bath, the resulting mixture was dispersed in 10mL of distilled water and then dialyzed with cellulose ester membranes of pore size 100–500Da (Biotech CE membrane) in order to remove any precursor from the reaction medium.

The resulting dialyzed solution was then ultra-filtered through a 50nm track-etched polycarbonate filter to remove the non-fluorescent aggregates. After freeze-drying the product, 238mg of a fine brown powder was obtained.

CNDs/Fluorescein sols were prepared by dissolving the desired amount (2.1mg, 6.4mmol) of fluorescein in 4.5mL of ethanol. After sonication (UltrasonicCleaner from VWR, 45kHz, 80W) of the solution for 10min at room temperature, methyltriethoxysilane (1.4mL, 7mmol) and diethoxydimethylsilane (0.84mL, 4.9mmol) were added to the solution.

After another 10min of sonication at room temperature, 1M acetic acid (25μL) and 1.3mL of the carbon dot stock solution diluted with distilled water to a final concentration of 0.12w/v% were added to the mixture.
The sol was then sonicated for another 20min and then aged for 24h at room temperature to yield the “aged sol”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PVDF-HFP electrospun fiber meshes
ce:section.ce:para: Poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropene) (PVDF-HFP) (Sigma Aldrich, Switzerland) with a molecular weight of 400kDa was dissolved in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (Sigma Aldrich, Switzerland, analytical grade) to obtain a 35% w/v solution. The electrospinning process was performed with a custom built device according to a procedure described elsewhere [25]. Briefly, a positive voltage of 14kV connected to the syringe and a negative voltage of 2kV connected to the plate collector were applied. The distance from the syringe to the collector was 20cm and a flow rate of 20μLmin−1 was used.

80μL of the “aged sol” were deposited on the center of circular PVDF-HFP electrospun fibre meshes (d=47mm). Afterwards, the deposited sol–gel films on the electrospun membranes were dried in the oven at 50°C for 48h.
A laboratory setup for testing the ammonia sensing probes was adapted from our previous setup[18] used for optical fibers (Fig. S-1).
Sol–gel films deposited onto PVDF-HFP electrospun fiber membrane were fixed onto the bottom of a flow cell.

The coatings were irradiated by a 0.4W UVLED of peak wavelength 365nm (FWHM bandwidth 10nm, purchased from Thorlabs) located at a 45° angle with respect to the functional coating. To measure fluorescence emission, the photodetector was placed orthogonal to the incoming radiation.

An optical band-pass filter was placed in front of the detector for the sensor calibration based on CNDs emission, which had a 90% transmission band in the range of 415–455nm (Semrock (FF02-435/40-25)). For the sensor calibration based on fluorescein emission, an optical band-pass filter was placed in front of the detector with a 90% transmission in the range of 500–550nm (Semrock (FF03-525/50-25)).

Electronic readout was based on a phototransistor circuit and a LabView-controlled data acquisition system (LabJack (U6b Series)). The phototransistor circuit included a low pass filter (cutoff frequency at 15.9Hz), an adjustable amplifier circuit (gain 1–300) with a potentiometer to set the operation point. The flow cell was connected to gas supplies and the gas flow could be switched between ammonia/nitrogen mixture and pure nitrogen using manual valves. During the experiments, the flow rate (2000mLmin−1 ±50mLmin−1), the temperature (23±2°C), and the relative humidity (<2%) were monitored in the line using a Mass Flow Meter for gases (Sensirion (CMOSens EM1)) and temperature and humidity data logger (MSR(MSR145)), respectively.

Electrospun membranes were analysed in terms of water contact angle, pore size and fiber diameter. Fiber diameters were determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images via image J processing software. 30 values were measured for 3 individual images of membranes produced in different batches. A Hitachi S-4800 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 2kV and 10μA current flow was used.

The water contact angle in air was assessed via a drop shape analyser DSA24 (Krüss GmbH, Hamburg, Germany). Five individual measurements were performed. Values for the contact angle were automatically calculated by the software of the device.

To determine the average pore size of the electrospun membranes, a capillary flow porometer Porolux 1000 (Porometer, Belgium) was used. The membranes were soaked in a perfluoroether wetting liquid (Porefil, Porometer, Belgium) with a low surface tension of 1.6×10−4 N/cm that was gradually displaced with increasing pressure during the measurement.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired on the FEI TECNAI F30 operated at 300kV. The TEM sample was prepared by dropping the carbon dot suspension onto a copper grid coated with ultra-thin carbon film before drying at room temperature under ambient conditions.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by collecting the purified GCD solution in water and freeze-drying for 24h. The measurements were performed on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe II instrument (USA) with a monochromatic AlKα X-ray source. Energy resolution of the spectrometer was set to 0.1eV/step and 23.50eV pass-energy for high resolution region scans. Carbon 1s at 284.5eV was used as a calibration reference to correct for charge effects. Elemental compositions were determined using instrument dependent atom sensitivity factors. Data analysis was performed by use of CasaXP software (Casa Software Ltd, United Kingdom).

Optical characterization of the CNDs was performed with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a multiwall-plate reader (Varian). 200μL of the GCD solution at a concentration of 0.5w/v% was deposited on a 96 well-plate and spectra emission recorded at different excitation wavelengths. When analyzing the effect of concentration on the optical properties, a fixed excitation wavelength of 360nm was utilized. In both cases, the excitation and emission slit width were kept constant at 5nm.

The FRET efficiency in solution was determined according to the following formula:
(1)
E = 1 −
I
D A
I D
where IDA is the integrated emission peak of the carbon dots within the liquid sol gel in the presence of fluorescein as FRET acceptor and ID is the integrated emission peak of the carbon nanodots within the liquid sol–gel in the absence of fluorescein. Different concentrations of CNDs were tested to evaluate the FRET efficiency in the presence and in the absence of 10v/v% of 0.1M NH4OH. The wavelength range employed for integration was 380 nm–475nm. For all samples, the excitation and emission slit width were kept constant at 5nm.

Sensor reversibility was tested by depositing 35μL of the sol–gel on customized PMMA plate (used instead of the PVDF-HFP electrospun membrane to offer a rigid transparent support) inserted in a black 24-well plate reader. Three PMMA slides were introduced as spacers from the bottom of the well plate in order to have the optimal access to the excitation beam. Subsequently, fluorescence measurements were performed with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a multiwall-plate reader (Varian). Conditioning of the sample to 50ppm of ammonia was attained by introducing the sample in an air-tight chamber in the presence of 78μL of 25% w/v NH3 solution and 300mL of water. This ensured that, based on the chamber volume, the overall ammonia partial pressure was 50ppm. The coated samples were placed in NH3 chamber/box for 1h to make sure the diffusion of NH3 in the solid-state sensor was completed.

Sensor cross-sensitivity was tested by depositing 200μL of the sol–gel solution on a transparent support made of poly(ethyleneterephtalate) (PET). Afterwards, the samples were placed in the oven at 50°C for two days. Then, the sensors were introduced diagonally within a quartz cuvette and exposed it to the desired level of humidity by conditioning in a controlled humidity environment for one hour. Alternatively, a solution of diluted HCl or TEA, whose concentration was corresponding to the targeted partial vapor pressures, was placed inside the cuvette and, after closing it up, the sensor was left to equilibrate for one hour.

Fluorescence measurements were recorded before and after exposure at an excitation wavelength of 360nm. In both cases, the excitation and emission slit width were kept constant at 5nm. The response was plotted as a function of the ratio between the integrated area of fluorescein (475–600nm) before and after introducing the interfering analyte (F/F0).

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesized by pyrolysis of EDTA in the presence of a 30wt.% solution of hydrogen peroxide at 250°C. HR-TEM images of the produced CNDs (Fig. 1 A) show narrow polydispersity with the average size being 2.1nm±0.2nm (from a total count of 72 nanoparticles). The average size is similar to materials obtained by pyrolysis of ethanolamine in the presence of hydrogen peroxide [23], although in this case the carbon dots showed a broader size distribution (i.e. 1.0-2.5nm) compared to the present study. The use of hydrogen peroxide have shown an increase of hydrophilic groups on the surface, which translated to a decrease in average size from 2.7nm to 2.0nm [23]. The same effect is observed if we compare CNDs observed in the present investigation with the ones previously synthesized by exclusively using EDTA [26].

The narrow particle size distribution (Fig. 1B) is comparable to CNDs synthesized by using a microfluidic device [27]. This is rather surprising if we consider that in the present bulk synthesis there is no control of the mixing region.

The reason behind such low polydispersity is probably related to the use of a strong oxidant such as hydrogen peroxide in the reaction medium although other factors, namely harsher reaction conditions, might play a role in the nucleation rate.

Hence, the presence of hydrogen peroxide contributes to the creation of higher electrostatic repulsion by increasing the polarity of the CNDs surface, which uniformly stabilizes the nanoclusters created during the carbonization process. Indeed, we have observed that the CNDs solution in de-ionized water at a concentration of 0.12w/v% displays a strong acidic pH value (e.g. 2.5).

To confirm this hypothesis, XPS analyses were performed (Fig. 1C–E). The relative concentration of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s were 56.7%, 17.8% and 25.6%, respectively. It is interesting to notice how the ratio oxygen/nitrogen decrease from 2:1 present in EDTA to 1.4:1 in the pristine CNDs, as a result of possible de-carboxylation phenomena occurring during the pyrolysis process. Moreover, the C 1s spectrum can be resolved in four different Gaussian peaks with maxima located at 284.5, 285.5, 286.9 and 288eV, which can be assigned to CC/CC, CN, COC/COH and CO/CN, respectively.

Hydrophilic groups (i.e. COC/COH, CN, CO/CN) observed in the C 1s spectrum were a marginal component compared to the CC/CC peak when CNDs were synthesized with EDTA in the absence of hydrogen peroxide [26]. In our case, CC/CC accounts only for 28% of the total C1s spectrum. This is a result of the combination between the oxidative conditions in the reaction medium caused by the presence of hydrogen peroxide and de-carboxylation phenomena occurring in non-inert atmosphere. Such finding is further confirmed by the O1s spectrum which shows only two peaks at 530.6eV and 532.2eV, indicative of the presence of CO and COC/COH bonds, respectively [26]. Moreover, in the N1s spectrum, two different peaks at 399.3eV and 400.8eV can be ascribed to CN and CN bonds, respectively [28].

The high surface coverage of hydrophilic groups is expected to facilitate their chemical (i.e. hydrogen bonding, electrostatic) interaction with ammonia, thereby promoting the decrease of the FRET inter-distance. Fluorescein may also display a significant interaction with ammonia in the gas phase thanks to the presence of the carboxylic functional group. This will translate into a higher FRET efficiency (as it is inversely proportional to the FRET inter-distance) and higher sensitivity of the resulting solid-state ammonia sensor.

A red-shift in the emission of carbon dots by increasing the excitation wavelength was confirmed by fluorescence measurements, with a maximum PL intensity observed in the excitation range of 360–380nm (Fig. 1F). This red-shifted emission at higher excitation wavelengths is one of the peculiar optical features of CNDs.

In the literature [29], such behavior has been mimicked by mixing three different polycyclic aromatics (PAHs), namely anthracene, pyrene and perylene. This phenomenon has been tentatively explained as a consequence of energy transfer processes occurring between different aromatic cores within the CNDs.

However, as another recent study [30] pointed out, other substituents also induce structural deformation to the aromatic cores, which leads to the formation of energy traps. The bandgap of each π→mid-gap state→π* can then vary in dependence of different heteroaromatic structures present in each CND’ aromatic core, which causes a variation of their emission states.

When considering the concentration effect on the CNDs optical properties (Fig. 1G), it can be observed that CNDs display aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ), which is a common feature observed among fluorophores. This is related to the increase of non-radiative internal conversion of the exciton caused by aggregation, which results in a decrease of the overall photoluminescence quantum yield. Their maximum in PL emission is located between 0.36w/v% and 0.60w/v%.

Furthermore, we noticed the appearance of two new emission peaks at 520nm and 574nm when using higher concentrations than 0.12w/v%. Formation of red-shifted emission peaks upon the use of concentrated CNDs solutions has recently been observed by Chen et al. [31]. The reason behind this phenomenon is probably related to the structural deformation in the molecular geometry of the aggregated aromatic cores induced by the exciton formation. Such deformation provokes the formation of self-trapped states, which can then stabilize the exciton and reduce its bandgap. This results in higher Stokes shifts with the appearance of the above-mentioned red-shifted peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological properties of electrospun membrane and the resulting deposited sol–gel
ce:section.ce:para: The PVDF-HFP electrospun membrane was synthesized according to a custom build device. The results showed an average fiber diameter of 690nm±150nm (Fig. 2 ). The polymer concentration as well as the type of solvent plays a crucial role for the final morphology of the membrane [32]. Based on our current results, using a 35% (w/v) solution of PVDF-HFP in DMF resulted in a homogenous and defect-free membrane. Lalia et al. [33] fabricated PVDF-HFP membranes from solutions dissolved in a mixture of acetone and dimethylacetamide. In their study, the homogeneity of the fiber diameters was inferior by direct comparison to our solvent system. After the deposition of the sol–gel film onto the fibers, an increase in fiber diameter to 1.26μm±0.28μm was observed. The increase in fiber diameter also influences the overall porosity of the membrane. An average pore size of 2.3μm was measured for the untreated PVDF-HFP membrane. Due to the addition of the functional sol–gel layer, the overall pore size of the electrospun membrane was reduced to values between 140 and 250nm with a broad distribution of the pore size (Fig. 2).

Hydrophobicity of the membrane was confirmed by water contact angle (WCA) measurements, which show an average WCA of 142.5±0.3°. Patel et al. [34] reported superhydrophobic properties with values above 150°. The differences can be explained by the larger fiber diameters reported in our study [35].
Indeed, assuming the Cassie-Baxter state (i.e. no water is penetrating hydrophobic membranes), this statement is in agreement with the theory described in the Cassie-Baxter equation [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response of the solid-state ammonia sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of our solid state ammonia sensor was tested in terms of sensitivity, reproducibility, reversibility and response time.

The response of three specimens of the sensor produced in separate batches with the same synthetic protocol was analyzed in order to evaluate the batch to batch reproducibility. Although the sensor can also perform in a ratiometric mode by calibrating as the ratio acceptor/donor emission, we have tested only individually the variation in intensity (e.g. I–I0) generated by the FRET donor (CNDs) and the FRET acceptor (fluorescein) as the result of exposure to different concentrations of NH3.

As it is depicted in Fig. 3 A, the sensor calibrated on the CNDs emission peak shows a stable calibration with excellent fitting to a power function (R2=0.99), with the intensity of its emission peak decreasing at higher ammonia concentrations. Despite the non-linearity of the fitting, the sensor can still be calibrated with a two-point method.

The non-linearity of the CNDs pH dependence can be related to the variety of internal and surface defects. Internal charge transfer (ICT) (in the case of internal defects) and photoelectron transfer (PET) (for protonated functional groups not directly conjugated to the emissive core) combined cause an averaged non-linear response. Such pH dependence has already been observed in a similar reaction by using ethanolamine and hydrogen peroxide as precursors [23]. On the other hand, by calibrating the signal with respect to the fluorescein emission peak, a sharp response to extremely low concentration of ammonia (i.e. 110 ppb) is observed (Fig. 5 B). In this case, the sensor shows a linear fitting within the tested dynamic range from 100ppb till 20ppm of ammonia, which is the relevant range for detecting ammonia exposure in occupational environments (Fig. 3B). Interestingly, by changing the optical filter and therefore targeting either the emission of CNDs or fluorescein, the sensor response can be calibrated for high ammonia concentration ranges (e.g. 20–200ppm) and low ammonia concentration ranges (e.g. 100ppb–20ppm).

Although fluorescein is well-known to increase its emission peak at higher pH values [18,37,38], such remarkable sharp response to ammonia cannot be exclusively ascribed to the fluorescein pH behavior. Indeed, as it has been observed by studying the FRET behavior in solution, a prominent increase in FRET efficiency from 85 to 88% is observed when 10v/v% of 0.1M NH4OH solution in water is added to the mixture (Figure S-3). Although such measurements give us a qualitative contribution of the energy transfer to the variation in signal of the fluorescein peak upon ammonia exposure, the translation of such efficiencies to the solid state sensor is not possible, as the FRET efficiency might diverge due to a different contribution of non-covalent interactions in the solid state.

Nevertheless, it can be stated that the fluorescein response to ammonia within the FRET pair is a combination of both factors: increased FRET efficiency due to reduced FRET inter-distance between the fluorophores and the pH-dependent behavior of fluorescein.

The reduced FRET inter-distance can be tentatively explained as a reversible formation of imine derivatives from the ketone groups observed on the CNDs surface, which render the CNDs surface prone to interact more strongly with the fluorescein carboxyl group, thereby establishing a stronger electrostatic interaction [39].

This translates into a lower limit of detection for the optical ammonia sensor (LODNH3). Indeed, the calculated LODNH3 (as the extrapolated concentration at which the signal is three times the averaged standard deviation of the readouts) resulted to be 110 ppb. Hence, the proposed sensor reduces by roughly three order of magnitudes the limit of detection obtained by the previous FRET pair evaluated by our group (i.e. 115ppm) [18]. This result so far represents the lowest limit of detection among the vast array of optical ammonia sensor works published in literature. Indeed, previous studies have shown limits of detection of 1.2ppm by utilizing CdSe/SiO2 semi-conductor quantum dots [12], 1.7ppm in the presence of red-emitting carbon dots [13] and 3ppm by using a FRET between carbon nanodots and sodium rhodizonate [22].

More importantly, the solid-state sensor presented here undergoes a completely reversible switching of the fluorescein optical signal up to six cycles upon exposure to 50ppm of ammonia (Fig. 4 ). Indeed, the relative standard deviation (RSD) at 50ppm obtained during the sensor calibration was 3.8%. However, the variation in fluorescence intensity between first and sixth cycle was only a mere 0.5%.

Surprisingly, not even minimal photo bleaching was observed after six cycles, although fluorescein is well-known to display poor photostability. The high reversibility represents an obvious advantage if compared to the previous FRET sensor based on carbon dots and sodium rhodizonate as in that case the reversibility was tested only upon the addition of gaseous HCl [22]. In our case, the strong hydrophobicity of the coating and the support membrane (water contact angle of 97.15° and 142.5°, respectively) drives the desorption of ammonia. The same effect cannot be achieved with more hydrophilic structures such as cotton fibers used as support in the previous study [22].

The response time of the sensors calibrated on the CNDs emission peak (defined as the time required to achieve 95% of the stable fluorescence intensity) shows a relatively long response time of 16±7min for the adsorption phase and 39±11min for the desorption phase (Figure S-4). Thus, CNDs are relatively slow to switch back to their original emissive state, probably because the kinetic of reversible dissociation of the imine into ketones and ammonia precludes any fast desorption.

However, the fluorescein response time is slightly faster, thereby proving that the interaction between fluorescein and CNDs weakens at a higher rate than the desorption of ammonia from CNDs (Fig. 5). Indeed, the t95% resulted to be 8±2min in adsorption and 15±2min in desorption upon exposure to 20ppm of ammonia, which represents a noticeable improvement if compared to our previous work [18], where even the thinnest sol–gel coating showed a relatively longer response time (i.e. 18±2min). Thus, the deposition of a sol–gel layer onto the electrospun fiber (localized either directly on the fiber or in the surroundings of several entangled fibers (Fig. 2)) increases the overall accessible surface area. This translates into a higher diffusion rate of the analyte and lower response time.

In addition, since the sol–gel film thickness tremendously affect the response time [18], we envisage the use of alternative processes for sol–gel deposition (i.e. dip coating or spin coating) or alternative matrix supports (e.g. polymer optical fibers (POFs)). With that, we expect a reduction of the inherent layer thickness, which will translate in an even faster response to ammonia. Consequently, a future study will focus on improving even further the response time and investigating selectivity (e.g. CO2/NH3 mixtures).
The response to interfering analytes, such as humidity, basic (e.g. trimethylamine) and acid (e.g. hydrochloric acid) vapors were tested to assess the sensor cross-sensitivity.

As it is depicted in Figure S-5A, the optical sensor shows a negligible response to humidity up to a RH level of 70%. Indeed, although ormosils are generally highly permeable to water due to their flexible siloxane bridges and weak intermolecular forces [40,41], the overall high hydrophobicity of the present sol–gel (e.g. WCA of 97.15°) allows a negligible cross-sensitivity to humidity.

Since the present sensor can be regarded as an optical pH sensor with higher sensitivity at basic pH values, hydrochloric acid and trimethylamine affect the sensor response (Figure S-5B). Indeed, the fluorescein signal is reduced by 56% upon exposure to 427ppm of HCl (related to HCl concentration of 20mol%) whereas exposure to 473ppm of TEA (related to TEA concentration of 0.55mol%) increases the fluorescein peak by 120%. Sensitivity to other organic volatile amines is a common feature for different types of ammonia sensors [42–44]. However, as it is shown here, the sensor response to high concentration of TEA vapor is much weaker if compared to ammonia, which can be ascribed to its bulkier structure, which strongly hampers the diffusion within the ormosil [37].

Cross-sensitivity to HCl has also been observed previously [22,42] and is related to the influence of HCl on the protonation degree of the functional groups on the surface of the CNDs and of the fluorescein carboxylic group.

It must be stressed out that our sensor is not designed to identify ammonia in a mixture of gases, but rather to detect dangerous concentrations of ammonia in controlled environments (occupational health). In these environments, the presence of other, interfering vapours can be neglected. Therefore, such cross-sensitivity, which is common to all optical-based ammonia detection methods, will not be critical for the intended application.

In this work, we demonstrated highly performing and fully reversible optical ammonia sensor based on FRET between fluorescein and carbon nanodots (CNDs). The integration of the FRET system into a flexible sol–gel coated PVDF/HFP electrospun fiber matrix allows an easy incorporation in the workers’ textiles for individual monitoring of ammonia in occupational environments.

From a mechanistic point of view, the presence of a strong oxidant in the synthesis of CNDs enhances the concentration of functional groups and reduces the average size and narrows down their polydispersity. This permits a strong interaction with the analyte, which results in a reduced FRET inter-distance and a lower limit of detection of the ammonia sensor (i.e. 110 ppb). The robustness, in terms of reproducibility and photostability, as well as the full reversibility of this ammonia sensor is appealing not only for the present system, but also for the development of alternative optical chemosensors. The low LOD achieved here may allow the future application of such sensors also in breath analysis for the non-invasive detection of renal or stomachal diseases, where detection levels of tenths to hundreds of ppb are required.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Lino Missaggia and the workshop team for the help in the design and construction of the gas sensor, Dr. Giuseppino Fortunato for his valuable contribution in the analysis of the XPS data, Dr. A. Osypova and the group of Dr. R. Erni in Dübendorf for preliminary analysis with the HR-TEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Light harvesting as a simple and versatile way to enhance brightness of luminescent sensors
Fast responsive, optical trace level ammonia sensor for environmental monitoring

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Luciano Fernandes Boesel obtained his PhD in Materials Science and Technology from the Univ. of Minho (Portugal) in 2006. He then worked several years with biomaterials and biopolymers (hydrogels, biodegradable polymers) and joined Empa as a Scientist in 2010. Since 2014 he leads the group “Adaptive Textiles and Hydrogels”, where his main focus are on the non-invasive body monitoring with optical fibers or fluorescent textiles and development of responsive transdermal delivery systems.

Dr. Claudio Toncelli obtained his PhD in Product Technology from the University of Groningen in 2011. After one year in a biomedical polymer company, he joined as post-doctoral researcher at the Biochemistry Department of University College Cork in 2013. He is currently the group leader in “Optofluidics” within the Laboratory of Biomimetic Membranes & Textiles at Empa. His expertise includes the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their encapsulation in multi-functional fibers for the development of wearable sensors.
